title: GitHub Actions - CI Basics
summary: Introduction to GitHub Actions for Continuous Integration. Learn to create workflows that automatically build, test, and validate your code on every push. Understand YAML workflow syntax, triggers, jobs, and steps.

quiz.title: Day 13 Quiz: GitHub Actions CI Basics

questions.questionText: Where must GitHub Actions workflow files be stored?
questions.explanation: GitHub Actions workflow files must be YAML files stored in the '.github/workflows' directory of your repository. GitHub automatically discovers and runs them.

questions.questionText: What does 'on: [push, pull_request]' mean in a workflow file?
questions.explanation: The 'on' key defines workflow triggers. '[push, pull_request]' means the workflow runs when code is pushed to the repository OR when a pull request is opened/updated.

options.text: The workflow runs only when both push and PR happen simultaneously
The workflow is triggered by either a push or a pull request event
The workflow pushes code and creates a pull request
The workflow runs on a schedule

questions.questionText: What is a 'step' in a GitHub Actions job?
questions.explanation: A step is an individual task within a job. It can either run a shell command (using 'run:') or use a pre-built action (using 'uses:'). Steps run sequentially within a job.

options.text: An individual task that runs a command or uses an action

questions.questionText: What does 'uses: actions/checkout@v4' do in a workflow?
questions.explanation: The 'actions/checkout' action checks out your repository code onto the runner, making it available for subsequent steps. Without it, the runner has no access to your code.

options.text: Checks out the repository code onto the runner

tasks.title: Create Your First CI Workflow
tasks.description:
1. In your GitHub repository, create the workflow directory:
   ```
   mkdir -p .github/workflows
   ```
2. Create a file `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with:
   ```yaml
   name: CI
   on:
     push:
       branches: [main]
     pull_request:
       branches: [main]
   jobs:
     build:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - name: List files
           run: ls -la
         - name: Run tests
           run: |
             echo 'Running tests...'
             # Add your test commands here
             echo 'All tests passed!'
         - name: Check shell scripts
           run: |
             if ls *.sh 1>/dev/null 2>&1; then
               bash -n *.sh
               echo 'Shell script syntax check passed'
             else
               echo 'No shell scripts found'
             fi
   ```
3. Commit and push to GitHub
4. Go to the Actions tab to see your workflow run
5. Click into the run to see the step-by-step output
hints: YAML is indentation-sensitive — use spaces, not tabs (2-space indent is standard)
The pipe '|' in YAML allows multi-line strings for 'run:' commands

tasks.title: Add Linting and Multi-Job Workflow
tasks.description:
1. Enhance your CI workflow with multiple jobs:
   ```yaml
   name: CI Pipeline
   on: [push, pull_request]
   jobs:
     lint:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - name: Install ShellCheck
           run: sudo apt-get install -y shellcheck
         - name: Lint shell scripts
           run: find . -name '*.sh' -exec shellcheck {} +
     test:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       needs: lint
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - name: Run tests
           run: echo 'Running tests after lint passed'
   ```
2. Note the `needs: lint` — this makes 'test' wait for 'lint' to pass
3. Add a shell script with a deliberate issue to see the linter catch it
4. Fix the issue and push again
5. Observe how jobs run sequentially vs in parallel
6. Add a status badge to your README:
   ```
   ![CI](https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/YOUR_REPO/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)
   ```
hints: Jobs without 'needs' run in parallel by default
Use 'continue-on-error: true' on a step if you want the workflow to continue even if that step fails
